name: Helm of Hades
description: The Helm of Hades, also known as the Cap of Invisibility, is a powerful artifact from Greek mythology. Forged by the Cyclopes, the one-eyed giants, it was gifted to Hades, the god of the Underworld, after they were freed from Tartarus by Zeus, Poseidon, and Hades themselves. This helm possesses the extraordinary ability to render its wearer completely invisible, shielding them from the sight of both gods and mortals. Its power extends beyond simple camouflage; it grants the user a veil of shadow, allowing them to move undetected and unseen, influencing events from the periphery. The helm played a significant role in various myths, most notably aiding Perseus in his quest to slay Medusa, allowing him to approach the Gorgon without being turned to stone by her gaze. Athena provided Perseus with the helm, understanding its immense power and strategic advantage. The Helm of Hades is a symbol of power, stealth, and the unseen forces that shape the world, representing the hidden influence and the ability to operate beyond the constraints of visibility and knowledge. It embodies the darkness and mystery associated with the Underworld and its ruler, Hades, highlighting the god's capacity to remain aloof and detached from the affairs of the living. The very presence of the helm evokes a sense of dread and the potential for unseen danger, a constant reminder of the power wielded by the unseen and unknown.
subtitle: Cap of Invisibility, Shadow Veil
origin: Forged by the Cyclopes for Hades

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus used the Helm of Hades, provided by Athena, to become invisible and approach Medusa without being petrified, allowing him to behead her. This was crucial for his success in the quest.

keyMyths.title: Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: It is implied the Helm played a role in the Titanomachy, the war between the Olympian gods and the Titans, as Hades was able to move undetected through the battlefield. It granted the gods an advantage.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Mentions the Cyclopes forging items for the gods, including possibly the Helm of Hades.
sources.text: Mentions the Cyclopes forging items for the gods, including possibly the Helm of Hades.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Describes Perseus's use of the Helm of Hades to defeat Medusa.
sources.text: Describes Perseus's use of the Helm of Hades to defeat Medusa.
